preamble.summary: A psalm of Asaph that moves from personal anguish to cosmic memory. The psalmist cries out to God at night, his spirit fainting, his hands stretched out without rest. He torments himself with questions: Has God rejected forever? Has His faithful love ceased? Has He forgotten mercy? Then the psalm pivots sharply at verse 11: 'I will remember the deeds of the LORD.' The second half recounts God's power at the Red Sea — thunder, lightning, shaking earth, waters writhing in fear — culminating in the declaration that God led His people like a flock through the hand of Moses and Aaron.
preamble.remarkable: Psalm 77 is constructed around a dramatic psychological pivot. Verses 2-10 describe a crisis so severe that even the act of remembering God causes pain (verse 4, 'I think of God and I groan'). The psalmist's questions in verses 8-10 are among the most daring in the Psalter — he asks whether God's chesed has permanently ceased, whether God's promise has failed, whether God has forgotten how to be gracious. These are not rhetorical questions; they feel like genuine, agonized uncertainty. Then verse 11 turns everything with the word ezkorah ('I will remember'). The same faculty that produced pain (remembering God and groaning) becomes the instrument of rescue (remembering God's deeds). Memory itself is redeemed.
preamble.friction: The Hebrew text of verse 11 contains a significant textual note. The word shanot ('years' or 'the changing of') has been read as both 'the years of the right hand of the Most High' and 'the changing (i.e., the failing) of the right hand of the Most High.' The Masoretic marginal note identifies this as a verse boundary between crisis and resolution: the first reading belongs to the lament, the second to the turning point. We follow the Masoretic verse division. The theophany in verses 17-20 draws on the same storm-god imagery found in Canaanite religion and in other biblical theophanies (Judges 5, Habakkuk 3, Psalm 18).
preamble.connections: The Red Sea theophany connects to Exodus 14-15, Habakkuk 3:8-15, and Psalm 114. The questions about God's chesed (verse 9) echo the crisis of Psalm 89:50 and Lamentations 3:22. The resolution through memory anticipates Psalm 78's extended historical retrospective. The final verse mentioning Moses and Aaron connects to Psalm 99:6 and the priestly-prophetic tradition. The storm theophany connects to Psalm 18:8-16 and Psalm 29.

verses.text_hebrew: לַמְנַצֵּ֥חַ עַל־יְדוּת֗וּן מִזְמ֥וֹר לְאָסָֽף׃
verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician, to Jeduthun, A Psalm of Asaph.
verses.rendering: For the director of music. According to Jeduthun. A psalm of Asaph.
translator_notes: Jeduthun was one of the three chief musicians appointed by David alongside Asaph and Heman (1 Chronicles 25:1-6). The phrase al yedutun may mean 'in the manner of Jeduthun' or 'to the tune of Jeduthun' — indicating a musical tradition associated with this Levitical guild.

verses.text_hebrew: קוֹלִ֣י אֶל־אֱלֹהִ֣ים וְאֶצְעָ֑קָה קוֹלִ֥י אֶל־אֱ֝לֹהִ֗ים וְהַאֲזִ֥ין אֵלָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: I cried unto God with my voice, even unto God with my voice; and he gave ear unto me.
verses.rendering:
My voice is to God, and I cry out;
my voice is to God — may He hear me!
verses.expanded_rendering: The doubled qoli el Elohim ('my voice to God') creates urgency through repetition. The psalmist is not whispering; he is crying aloud (ve-ets'aqah, from tsa'aq, the verb used for the Israelites' cry under Egyptian slavery in Exodus 2:23). The second line — veha'azin elai ('and may He give ear to me') — is either a statement of confidence ('He heard me') or a plea ('may He hear me'). The ambiguity captures the psalm's tension: the psalmist both trusts and doubts.
translator_notes: The verb tsa'aq ('to cry out') is the specific vocabulary of distress-calling in the Hebrew Bible — it is the cry of the oppressed to a superior who has the power to intervene. It is not prayer as meditation but prayer as emergency alert.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּי֥וֹם צָרָתִ֨י ׀ אֲדֹנָ֣י דָּרָ֗שְׁתִּי יָדִ֤י ׀ לַ֤יְלָה נִגְּרָ֗ה וְלֹ֣א תָפ֑וּג מֵאֲנָ֖ה הִנָּחֵ֣ם נַפְשִֽׁי׃
verses.text_kjv: In the day of my trouble I sought the Lord: my sore ran in the night, and ceased not: my soul refused to be comforted.
verses.expanded_rendering: The phrase beyom tsarati ('in the day of my distress') marks the crisis. The verb darashti ('I sought') implies diligent, persistent searching — not a casual prayer but an intensive quest. The image yadi laylah niggerah ('my hand was poured out/stretched out at night') describes hands raised in prayer all night without ceasing. The verb velo tafug ('it did not go numb, it did not flag') means his hands never dropped. And yet: me'anah hinnachem nafshi ('my soul refused to be comforted'). The psalmist prayed all night and received no relief. The honesty is startling.
translator_notes: The verb niggerah is often emended to niggarah ('was poured out') or read as 'streamed' (referring to tears). The Masoretic pointing supports 'was stretched out' — hands extended upward in the posture of supplication throughout the night.

verses.text_hebrew: אֶזְכְּרָ֣ה אֱלֹהִ֣ים וְאֶהֱמָיָ֑ה אָשִׂ֓יחָה ׀ וְתִתְעַטֵּ֖ף רוּחִ֣י סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: I remembered God, and was troubled: I complained, and my spirit was overwhelmed. Selah.
verses.rendering:
I think of God and I groan;
I meditate, and my spirit faints. Selah.
translator_notes: The verb hamah ('to groan, to murmur, to be in turmoil') describes an involuntary sound of distress — it is what happens when thought becomes unbearable and the body responds with noise. The verb ataf ('to be faint, to be wrapped, to be enveloped') suggests being smothered or overwhelmed.

verses.text_hebrew: אָ֭חַזְתָּ שְׁמֻר֣וֹת עֵינָ֑י נִ֝פְעַ֗מְתִּי וְלֹ֣א אֲדַבֵּֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou holdest mine eyes waking: I am so troubled that I cannot speak.
verses.rendering:
You hold my eyelids open;
I am so troubled that I cannot speak.
translator_notes: The attribution of insomnia to God is a bold theological move — it means God is not merely absent but actively involved in the psalmist's suffering. This is harder to bear than divine indifference.

verses.text_hebrew: חִשַּׁ֥בְתִּי יָמִ֗ים מִ֫קֶּ֥דֶם שְׁ֝נ֗וֹת עוֹלָמִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: I have considered the days of old, the years of ancient times.
verses.rendering:
I consider the days of old,
the years of ages past.
translator_notes: The psalmist reaches backward: yamim mi-qedem ('days from of old') and shenot olamim ('years of eternity'). He is searching history for precedent — has God ever acted like this before? Was there a time when God was faithful? The search through memory is both an act of desperation and a prelude to the resolution that comes in verses 11-12.

verses.text_hebrew: אֶ֥זְכְּרָ֗ה נְגִ֫ינָתִ֥י בַּלָּ֑יְלָה עִם־לְבָבִ֥י אָ֝שִׂ֗יחָה וַיְחַפֵּ֥שׂ רוּחִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: I call to remembrance my song in the night: I commune with mine own heart: and my spirit made diligent search.
verses.expanded_rendering: The word neginati ('my song, my melody') may refer to psalms the psalmist used to sing in better times — the memory of past worship becomes another source of pain. The phrase im levavi asichah ('with my heart I meditate') describes internal dialogue — the psalmist is interrogating himself. The verb vayechapes ('it searches, it investigates') presents the spirit (ru'ach) as a detective trying to solve the problem of God's apparent abandonment.
translator_notes: The progression from external cry (verse 2) to internal meditation (verse 7) shows the psalmist moving deeper inward, trying every avenue of approach to the problem. Neither outward prayer nor inward reflection has produced resolution.

verses.text_hebrew: הַלְעוֹלָמִ֥ים יִזְנַ֥ח ׀ אֲדֹנָ֑י וְלֹ֤א יֹסִ֖יף לִרְצֹ֣ת עֽוֹד׃
verses.text_kjv: Will the Lord cast off for ever? and will he be favourable no more?
verses.rendering:
Will the Lord reject forever?
Will He never again show favor?
translator_notes: These questions are not answered within the psalm. The resolution comes not from answers but from a change in the direction of memory — from remembering personal suffering to remembering God's historical acts.

verses.text_hebrew: הֶאָפֵ֣ס לָנֶ֣צַח חַסְדּ֑וֹ גָּ֥מַר אֹ֝֗מֶר לְדֹ֣ר וָדֹֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: Is his mercy clean gone for ever? doth his promise fail for evermore?
verses.rendering:
Has His faithful love ceased forever?
Has His promise come to an end for all generations?
verses.expanded_rendering: The third question strikes at the heart: he-afes lanetsach chasdo ('has His chesed come to nothing forever?'). The word afes means 'to cease, to come to an end, to run out.' If God's chesed — His covenant loyalty, His faithful love, His steadfast commitment — has run out, then everything is lost. Chesed is the adhesive of the covenant; without it, the relationship dissolves. The fourth question: gamar omer ledor vador ('has His word/promise been finished for all generations?'). The verb gamar ('to finish, to complete, to end') treats God's promise as a finite resource that can be exhausted.
translator_notes: The word chesed is one of the most important words in the Hebrew Bible and resists single-word translation. It combines loyalty, love, faithfulness, kindness, and covenant commitment. To ask whether chesed has ceased is to ask whether God's fundamental character has changed.

key_terms.semantic_range: faithful love, loyal kindness, covenant devotion, steadfast love, mercy, lovingkindness
key_terms.note: Chesed is God's self-binding commitment to those in covenant with Him. It is not mere emotion but covenantal action — God acting faithfully because He has bound Himself to do so. The psalmist's question about whether chesed has ceased is the most dangerous theological question possible: it challenges God's reliability.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲשָׁכַ֣ח חַנּ֣וֹת אֵ֑ל אִם־קָפַ֥ץ בְּ֝אַ֗ף רַחֲמָ֥יו סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: And I said, This is my infirmity: but I will remember the years of the right hand of the most High.
verses.rendering:
Has God forgotten to be gracious?
Has He in anger shut up His compassion? Selah.
verses.expanded_rendering: The fifth and final question: hashakhach channot El ('has God forgotten to be gracious?'). The verb shakhach ('to forget') applied to God is theologically explosive — God does not forget (Isaiah 49:15). Yet the psalmist asks it. The parallel — im qafats be-af rachamav ('has He in anger closed His compassion?') — pictures God's rachamim ('compassion, womb-love') as something that can be sealed shut. The verb qafats ('to close, to draw together, to shut') suggests a deliberate act: God has chosen to compress and contain His compassion. The selah provides space for these terrible questions to hang in the air.
translator_notes: The word rachamim ('compassion') derives from rechem ('womb') — it is the visceral, maternal feeling of tenderness. To ask whether God has shut His rachamim is to ask whether the most instinctive form of divine love has been suppressed.

verses.text_hebrew: וָ֭אֹמַר חַלּוֹתִ֣י הִ֑יא שְׁ֝נ֗וֹת יְמִ֣ין עֶלְיֽוֹן׃
verses.text_kjv: And I said, This is my infirmity: but I will remember the years of the right hand of the most High.
verses.rendering:
And I said, 'This is my grief:
that the right hand of the Most High has changed.'
verses.expanded_rendering: This verse is the hinge of the psalm. The word challoti ('my grief, my wound, my sickness') names the spiritual affliction. The phrase shenot yemin Elyon is the most debated phrase in the psalm: shenot can mean 'years of' or 'the changing of.' Reading it as 'the changing of the right hand of the Most High' means the psalmist's deepest pain is his perception that God has changed — that the powerful right hand that once saved now withholds. This interpretation connects to the questions of verses 8-10: if God's chesed has ceased, His promise has ended, and His compassion is shut — then the right hand of the Most High has changed.
translator_notes: The Masoretic tradition places a verse break here that separates the lament (verses 2-11a) from the resolution (verses 11b-21). Some scholars read shenot as 'years' and connect it to verse 12: 'I will remember the years of the right hand of the Most High' — making it the first statement of recovery rather than the last statement of despair. Both readings are theologically coherent.

verses.text_hebrew: אֶזְכּ֥וֹר מַֽעַלְלֵי־יָ֑הּ כִּֽי־אֶזְכְּרָ֖ה מִקֶּ֣דֶם פִּלְאֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: I will remember the works of the LORD: surely I will remember thy wonders of old.
verses.rendering:
I will remember the deeds of the LORD;
yes, I will remember Your wonders of old.
translator_notes: The doubled 'I will remember' (ezkor... ezkerah) mirrors the doubled 'my voice to God' in verse 2. The psalm's structure is built on repetition with redirection: the same faculties (voice, memory) that expressed the crisis now express the resolution.

verses.text_hebrew: וְהָגִ֥יתִי בְכׇל־פׇּעֳלֶ֑ךָ וּ֝בַעֲלִ֥ילוֹתֶ֗יךָ אָשִֽׂיחָה׃
verses.text_kjv: I will meditate also of all thy work, and talk of thy doings.
verses.rendering:
I will meditate on all Your work
and reflect on Your mighty deeds.
translator_notes: The verb hagiti ('I will meditate, I will murmur') is the same verb used in Psalm 1:2 for meditating on Torah. The psalmist turns his restless, sleepless mind toward a new subject: God's po'olekha ('Your work') and alilotekha ('Your deeds, Your exploits'). The cure for destructive meditation is redirected meditation.

verses.text_hebrew: אֱ‍ֽלֹהִ֗ים בַּקֹּ֥דֶשׁ דַּרְכֶּ֑ךָ מִי־אֵ֥ל גָּ֝ד֗וֹל כֵּֽאלֹהִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Thy way, O God, is in the sanctuary: who is so great a God as our God?
verses.rendering:
Your way, O God, is holy.
What god is as great as our God?
verses.expanded_rendering: The word baqqodesh can mean 'in holiness' or 'in the sanctuary.' If 'in holiness,' the claim is about God's character — His way is set apart, unlike any other. If 'in the sanctuary,' the claim is about location — God's way is discerned in worship, which is exactly what happened to the psalmist of Psalm 73. The rhetorical question mi El gadol ke-Elohim ('what god is as great as God?') echoes the Song of the Sea (Exodus 15:11) and declares God's incomparability.
translator_notes: The echo of Exodus 15:11 ('Who is like You among the gods, O LORD?') is deliberate — the psalmist is moving toward the Red Sea memory, and the language of the Song of the Sea begins to surface.

verses.text_hebrew: אַתָּ֣ה הָ֭אֵל עֹ֣שֵׂה פֶ֑לֶא הוֹדַ֖עְתָּ בָעַמִּ֣ים עֻזֶּֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou art the God that doest wonders: thou hast declared thy strength among the people.
verses.rendering:
You are the God who does wonders;
You have made Your strength known among the peoples.
translator_notes: The phrase oseh fele ('who does wonders') is a title — it defines God by His characteristic action. The verb hoda'ta ('You have made known') means God's power is not hidden but publicly displayed ba-ammim ('among the peoples') — not just Israel but the nations.

verses.text_hebrew: גָּ֭אַלְתָּ בִּזְר֣וֹעַ עַמֶּ֑ךָ בְּנֵֽי־יַעֲקֹ֖ב וְיוֹסֵ֣ף סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou hast with thine arm redeemed thy people, the sons of Jacob and Joseph. Selah.
verses.rendering:
You redeemed Your people with Your arm —
the children of Jacob and Joseph. Selah.
verses.expanded_rendering: The verb ga'alta ('You redeemed') is kinsman-redeemer language — God bought His people back from slavery. The zero'a ('arm') is God's power in action (the same term as Psalm 71:18). The naming of both Ya'aqov ('Jacob') and Yosef ('Joseph') is unusual — it may represent the southern tribes (Jacob/Judah) and the northern tribes (Joseph/Ephraim), a way of naming all Israel. The selah marks the transition from history to theophany.
translator_notes: The mention of Joseph alongside Jacob suggests the psalmist has the entire twelve-tribe nation in view, not just Judah. Joseph was the ancestor of Ephraim and Manasseh, the dominant northern tribes.

verses.text_hebrew: רָ֘א֤וּךָ מַּ֨יִם ׀ אֱ‍ֽלֹהִ֗ים רָא֣וּךָ מַּ֣יִם יָחִ֑ילוּ אַ֝֗ף יִרְגְּז֥וּ תְהֹמֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: The waters saw thee, O God, the waters saw thee; they were afraid: the depths also were troubled.
verses.expanded_rendering: The theophany begins with a startling personification: the waters (mayim) see God and react with terror. The doubled ra'ukha mayim ('the waters saw You') creates emphasis — the waters see, and they writhe (yachilu, from chul, 'to writhe in pain, to twist'). The word tehomot ('the deeps, the primordial depths') is the same word as tehom in Genesis 1:2 — the deep before creation. Even the primordial ocean trembles before God. This is Exodus language (the sea parting) merged with creation language (the deep responding to the Creator).
translator_notes: The personification of water 'seeing' God and responding with terror is one of the most powerful images in the Psalter. Water, in the ancient Near Eastern worldview, represented chaos — and here chaos itself is afraid of God.

verses.text_hebrew: זֹ֤רְמוּ מַ֨יִם ׀ עָב֗וֹת ק֭וֹל נָתְנ֣וּ שְׁחָקִ֑ים אַף־חֲ֝צָצֶ֗יךָ יִתְהַלָּֽכוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: The clouds poured out water: the skies sent out a sound: thine arrows also went abroad.
translator_notes: The theophany builds: zorm means 'to pour, to stream' — the clouds become waterfalls. The shekhaqim ('skies, clouds') give qol ('voice, thunder'). God's chatsatsekha ('Your arrows') are lightning bolts that yithallakhu ('go about, fly in all directions'). The storm is God's chariot, His weapon, His mode of transport.

verses.text_hebrew: ק֤וֹל רַעַמְךָ֨ ׀ בַּגַּלְגַּ֗ל הֵאִ֣ירוּ בְרָקִ֣ים תֵּבֵ֑ל רָגְזָ֖ה וַתִּרְעַ֣שׁ הָאָֽרֶץ׃
verses.text_kjv: The voice of thy thunder was in the heaven: the lightnings lightened the world: the earth trembled and shook.
verses.expanded_rendering: The qol ra'amkha ('the voice of Your thunder') was bagalgal ('in the whirlwind, in the rolling wheel'). The word galgal suggests both a wheel and a rolling storm — thunder that circles and reverberates. Lightning (beraqim) illuminates the entire tevel ('world, inhabited earth'). The earth responds: ragzah ('it trembled, it quaked') and vattir'ash ('it shook'). The created order shudders under the weight of God's passing. This is not weather but theophany — God Himself moving through the storm.
translator_notes: The word galgal is sometimes translated 'heaven' or 'whirlwind.' Its root meaning is 'something that rolls' — a wheel, a whirlwind, a rolling thunder. The image is of God's voice rolling across the sky like a wheel of sound.

verses.text_hebrew: בַּיָּ֤ם דַּרְכֶּ֗ךָ וּ֭שְׁבִֽילְךָ בְּמַ֣יִם רַבִּ֑ים וְ֝עִקְּבוֹתֶ֗יךָ לֹ֣א נֹדָֽעוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thy way is in the sea, and thy path in the great waters, and thy footsteps are not known.
verses.rendering:
Your way was through the sea,
Your path through the mighty waters,
and Your footprints were not known.
verses.expanded_rendering: This is the psalm's most profound verse. God's way (darkekha) went through the sea — the Red Sea, where Israel crossed. His path (shevilekkha) went through the great waters (mayim rabbim). But the final phrase is the key: ve-iqqevotekha lo noda'u ('and Your footprints were not known'). God walked through the sea, but when the waters closed behind Him, the footprints vanished. There is no evidence, no proof, no trace. God acted decisively and then covered His tracks. The psalmist must believe without visible evidence — and this is precisely the psalm's resolution. Faith does not rest on footprints but on memory of deeds.
translator_notes: The invisible footprints are a metaphor for divine hiddenness: God acts, but He does not leave the kind of evidence that eliminates the need for faith. The psalmist in verses 2-10 was looking for visible signs and finding none; now he understands that God's way has always been through waters that close behind Him.

verses.text_hebrew: נָחִ֣יתָ כַצֹּ֣אן עַמֶּ֑ךָ בְּיַד־מֹשֶׁ֥ה וְאַהֲרֹֽן׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou leddest thy people like a flock by the hand of Moses and Aaron.
verses.rendering:
You led Your people like a flock
by the hand of Moses and Aaron.
translator_notes: The abrupt ending is deliberate. The psalm does not resolve into a statement of certainty about the present — it resolves into a memory of the past and an implied trust for the future. The psalmist's insomnia, his questions, his anguish — none of them are explicitly answered. What changes is the direction of his gaze: from inward suffering to backward memory to forward trust.
